messages.content:
To prevent the option to call remove or grant access if the user type is owner, you can add an additional condition to the `disabled` prop of the `<Menu>` component. Update the `disabled` prop as follows:

```jsx
disabled={Boolean((row.user_type === 'ADMIN' && user?.accessLevel === 'LEAD') || (row.user_type === 'OWNER' && user?.accessLevel !== 'OWNER')) || row.user_type === 'OWNER'}
```

This added condition `row.user_type === 'OWNER'` checks if the user type is "OWNER" and disables the menu options if it evaluates to true.
i simply want to remove the entire option

To get the `uuid` of the option with the `name_en` of "Other - Specify" from the `industryOptions` array, you can use the `find` method. Here's an example:

```jsx
const desiredOption = industryOptions.find(option => option.name_en === "Other - Specify");
const uuid = desiredOption?.uuid; // "edf271bc-ea2e-4409-8ae0-d8bc051c7725"
```

In this code, the `find` method is used to search for an option where the `name_en` property matches "Other - Specify". The `desiredOption` will be the object that matches the condition, and then you can access its `uuid` property using `desiredOption?.uuid`.
what  if industryOptions was null would that cause an issue using .find?

why i have this on my code: his expression is not callable.
  Type 'String' has no call signatures.ts

The error message "This expression is not callable. Type 'String' has no call signatures" suggests that you are trying to call a string as if it were a function.

This error often occurs when you mistakenly try to call a non-function value as if it were a function. For example, if you try to call a string variable as a function:

```jsx
const myString = "Hello";
myString();
```

To resolve this error, make sure that you are not mistakenly trying to call a string as a function. Double-check your code to ensure that you are correctly using function calls. If the error persists or if you can provide more specific code, I'd be happy to assist you further.
I have this code: const [setOtherId, otherId] =useState<string>("none");

and this  const desiredOption = industryOptions ? industryOptions.find(option => option.name_en === "Other - Specify") : null;      
        setOtherId(desiredOption?.uuid || "none");
yes it is on the useState since it is the setter of otherId,  what is the mistake im doing here

Apologies for the confusion. Upon reviewing the code you provided again, it seems that there is no mistake in how you are using the `useState` hook. The error message you are encountering, "This expression is not callable. Type 'String' has no call signatures.ts(2349)," might not be related to the code snippet you shared.

The error message typically occurs when you attempt to call a value that is not a function. It might be caused by other parts of your code that accidentally call the `setOtherId` variable as a function, although it is defined as a string in your code snippet.

To further troubleshoot the issue, please provide more context or the specific code that triggers the error. It will help in identifying the root cause and addressing the problem more accurately.
